articles.title: Birthday Congratulations to Amy Brown Lyman
articles.text:
IT has been well said that life is enriched and fulfilled by the development of one's inherent abilities, and by a reaching out into the lives of others to uplift and comfort them. Sister Amy Brown Lyman, since her early youth, has exemplified in a wide field of service this spirit of dedication and devotion. As a young woman she became interested in Relief Society by observing the spiritual solace and practical ministrations given by the sisters who were joined together in love and service to each other and to their communities. Sister Lyman has long served Relief Society in many capacities — as General Secretary-Treasurer, as First Counselor in the General Presidency, and as General President. She continues to serve as a ward literature class leader.

Relief Society women throughout the Church are grateful for the continuing influence of her inspiration and far-reaching leadership. The General Board and the members of the world-wide sisterhood extend birthday congratulations to a beloved leader and wish her much happiness.

articles.title: Faced Underarm Placket
articles.text:
A faced or a faced and hemmed placket gives a flat, smooth closing with snaps or hooks. It may be used on light wools or silks in situations where zippers are not practical or not desired. It is also frequently used on the bottom of tight-fitting sleeves.

In making this placket, both front and back edges are faced with a lengthwise strip of matching fabric. Stitch, press, and edge finish the side and waistline seams then:

1. Cut two strips on the lengthwise grain 1½ inches wide and 2 inches longer than the opening.

2. Place the strips on the front and back placket edges with right sides together. Using a ¼ inch seam allowance, and leaving one inch above and below placket ends, place the strip for the front edge so that it can be stitched on the seam line of the garment. The strip for the back edge can be stitched at least ½ inch from the side seam line. Stitch the strips to the garment backstitching at the ends.

3. Steam press the placket seams open and trim allowances on facings to ¼ inch. Allowances on a dress may be left wider for possible alteration. Turn facings to the wrong side and press well to insure thin edges along the opening.

4. Finish raw edges to prevent fraying.

5. Anchor seam tape behind front facing through which to sew snaps and hooks and eyes. Fasten front facing to dress with an invisible hemming stitch.

6. On the back edge, clip through seam allowance at top and bottom so they will turn forward under front edge. Hand stitch to front at top and bottom. Overcast raw edges.

If seam allowances are wide enough, one or both raw edges may be hemmed back instead of faced. In this case edges must be reinforced with seam tape to prevent stretching.

Remember always that every placket should be made to fit as smoothly as a seam in order to retain a perfect fit.
